sets.name: The Ecosystem of Heritage Tourism
sets.description: Key terms, government agencies, laws/EOs, and stakeholder roles from the Heritage Tourism ecosystem lecture.

terms.definition: Individuals, groups, or organizations that have an interest in, are affected by, or can affect the development and management of heritage tourism.

terms.term: 3 Ways a Party Qualifies as a Stakeholder
answers: Has an interest in heritage tourism

terms.definition: Their role: the main reason for travel and the foundation of all tourism activity. Example: Intramuros.

answers: Act as consumers of heritage tourism products

terms.term: 4 Functions of Government Agencies in Heritage Tourism
answers: Protection of cultural and natural heritage

terms.definition: Their role: residents living within or near heritage sites who keep traditions, stories, and practices alive, and serve as front-line ambassadors.

terms.term: 4 Roles of NGOs and Public Interest Groups

terms.definition: Their role: link heritage sites with tourists by packaging, selling, and promoting experiences. Examples: travel agencies, tour operators, guides.

terms.term: 5 Categories of Suppliers and Service Providers

terms.definition: Created under P.D. 189; overall in charge of all tourism sites, attractions, activities, services, and infrastructure in the Philippines.
Previously called NHI; created by P.D. 260; in charge of preservation, restoration, and reconstruction of important historic sites, churches, national shrines, monuments, and landmarks.

terms.term: National Commission for Culture and the Arts
terms.definition: Mandated by R.A. 7356; formulates policies for the development of culture and the arts, and promotes and preserves Filipino cultural heritage.

terms.term: Tourism Infrastructure and Enterprise Zone Authority
terms.definition: Established under R.A. 9593; manages government-owned, tourism-oriented establishments usually located in tourist destinations.

terms.term: Department of Public Works and Highways
terms.definition: Important for providing land access to heritage sites and attractions.

terms.term: Department of Interior and Local Government
terms.definition: Under R.A. 6795; supervises all local government units (LGUs).
Created under P.D. 1616; preserves and enhances the historical value of Intramuros.
Created as an Executive Committee in 1963 under the Office of the President; tasked to develop and preserve the Quezon Memorial, Rizal Park, and other national parks.
Under R.A. 9593; serves as the marketing arm of the Department of Tourism.

terms.term: Department of Environment and Natural Resources
terms.definition: Under Section 4 of Executive Order 192; primary government agency for conservation, management, development, and proper use of the country's environment and natural resources.
Under EO 33; primary coordinative, promotive, and facilitative arm for trade, industry, and investment activities; supports small and micro enterprises in host communities.

terms.term: Bureau of Food and Drug Administration
terms.definition: Regulates food and drug safety in relation to tourism services.
Under EO 1037; tasked to address and supervise issues concerning retirees.
Under R.A. 1937; has power to assess and collect lawful revenues, prevent smuggling and other frauds, and enforce tariff and customs laws.
Under EO 292; serves as the immigration regulatory and control body of the Philippines.
Main agency tasked to maintain peace and order in communities around the country.
Under R.A. 5173; mandated to assist in enforcing all applicable laws upon the high seas and waters within Philippine jurisdiction.

answers: Acquires works of art, specimens, and cultural and historical artifacts
Fosters scholarly study and public appreciation of them

terms.definition: Agency responsible for protecting the rights of the indigenous peoples of the Philippines.

sets.name: Definition of Cooking Terms
sets.description: Fundamental culinary terms: cooking processes, technical terms, and French cooking terminology used in kitchen operations.

terms.definition: Cooked so it's still tough when bitten, often referring to pasta.

A container holding hot water into which a pan is placed for slow cooking, otherwise known as a "water bath" or "double boiler". A roasting pan or baking dish partially filled with water to allow food to cook more slowly and be protected from direct high heat. Used for custards and terrines.
To cook foods by surrounding them with hot, dry air. Similar to roast, but this term usually applies to breads, pastries, vegetables, and fish.

(1) To cook with dry heat created by the burning of hardwood or by the hot coals of this wood. (2) Loosely, to cook over hot coals, such as on a grill or spit, often with a seasoned marinade or basting sauce.
To mix ingredients rapidly to incorporate air.

To cook an item partially and very briefly in boiling water or in hot fat. Usually a pre-preparation technique, as to loosen peels of vegetables, fruits, and nuts, to partially cook french fries or other foods before service, to prepare for freezing, or to remove undesirable flavors.
To mix ingredients until smooth.
To cook in water or other liquid that is bubbling rapidly, about 212°F (100°C) at sea level and at normal pressure.
To cook certain vegetables slowly in a small amount of liquid without preliminary browning.

To cook food slowly in a small amount of liquid in a covered pot; a combination-cooking method that first sears the food at high temperature, then finishes it in a covered pot at low temperature while sitting in some amount of liquid.
To cook with radiant heat from above.
To cook submerged in hot fat.
To pour juices or melted fat over meat or other food while cooking to keep it moist.
A thick, creamy soup, with a base of strained broth of shellfish or game.
The process of soaking meat in heavily salted water before cooking, similar to marination.
To cut food into small, irregular pieces.
Shredded or finely cut vegetables and herbs, usually used as a garnish for soup.
A type of clear soup made from richly flavored stock that has been clarified, a process of using egg whites to remove fat.
To mix until smooth and fluffy (e.g., butter and sugar).
To swirl a liquid in a sauté pan, roast pan, or other pan to dissolve cooked particles of food remaining on the bottom.
To cut food into small, uniform cubes.
Methods in which heat is conducted to foods without the use of moisture.
To coat wet or moist foods with a dry ingredient before cooking to provide an even coating.
To put oil, vinegar, salt, or other toppings on a salad or other food.
A boneless piece of meat, poultry, or fish; the French version, spelled as "filet," is also used when referencing a cut of beef that is boneless, such as filet mignon.
The process of adding alcohol such as brandy, cognac, or rum to a hot pan to create a burst of flames.
To cook food in hot oil or fat.
The process of creating a shiny, translucent coating on food, often using a sauce or other liquid, to enhance flavor and appearance.
To cook food over direct heat, usually on a rack.
The process of extracting chemical compounds or flavors from a vegetable in water, oil, or alcohol, by allowing the material to remain suspended in the liquid over time, also known as steeping.
The process of inserting strips of fat into a piece of meat that doesn't have as much fat, to melt and keep the meat from drying out.
A binding agent of cream and egg yolks used to thicken soups or sauces.
To crush food until smooth.
The process of soaking foods in seasoned and acidic liquid before cooking for hours or days, adding flavor to the food.
To finely divide food into uniform pieces smaller than diced or chopped foods, prepared using a chef's knife or food processor.
The preparation of ingredients, such as dicing onions or measuring spices, before starting cooking.
The base sauce used to make other variations of the original sauce.
The process of adding foods to boiling waters, cooking until they are softened, then removing before they are fully cooked, usually to partially cook an item which will then be cooked another way.
The process of not fully cooking food, so that it can be finished or reheated later.
To cook food gently in liquid below boiling point.
To mash or blend until smooth.
To cook the fat out of something, such as bacon.
To cook food using dry heat, usually in an oven.
To cook food quickly over relatively high heat, literally meaning "to jump" as the food does when placed in a hot pan.
To heat a liquid so it's right about to reach the boiling point, where bubbles start to appear around the edges.
A technique used in grilling, baking, or sautéing in which the surface of the food is cooked at high temperature until a crust forms.
To remove foam or fat from liquid surface.

A cooking method that involves exposing food, typically meat, poultry, or fish, to smoke from burning or smoldering material, most often wood, to impart flavor, color, and potentially preserve the food.
To cook in liquid just below boiling point.
To cook food using the vapor from boiling water.
Gently heating vegetables in a little oil, with frequent stirring and turning to ensure emitted liquid will evaporate; usually results in tender, or in some cases such as onions', translucent pieces.
Raising the temperature of a cold or room-temperature ingredient by slowly adding hot or boiling liquid, often referring to eggs.
To make meat softer by pounding or marinating.
To tie meat or poultry, such as turkey with a string, woven through the bird parts by using a needle, in order to create a more compact shape before cooking.
A type of sauce in which a light stock, such as chicken or fish, is thickened with a flour that is cooked and then allowed to turn light brown; made from butter, flour, cream and stock.
To beat food with a mixer to incorporate air and produce volume, often used to create heavy cream, salad dressings, or sauces.
A cooking utensil used to blend ingredients in a process such as whipping.
Butter and flour mixed together in equal parts and used to thicken liquids.
Broth or stock.
A mixture of fresh herbs tied together with string and used to flavor stews, soups etc. It refers to a mix of parsley, bay leaf, thyme (and sometimes celery stalk). It is removed before serving.
Vegetables cut into very small diced pieces.
An appetizer consisting of a small bread or biscuit base covered with flavored topping.
The term used to refer to the actual main course.
Dessert or sweet.
First course or appetizer.
To cut food into thin, matchstick-like strips.
Small diced mixed vegetables, usually containing at least one root vegetable. Sometimes also means a mixture of fruit, like fruit salad.
A mixture of braising vegetables.
Translates to "in paper" or "in parchment". A French cooking term that refers to a method of cooking where food is enclosed in a folded packet of parchment paper or foil and then baked.
A sweet or pastry. Also refers to cake shop.
Melted butter which is added with flour. It is usually used as a thickener for sauces or soups.

sets.name: Food Preparation Tools and Equipment
sets.description: Cookware, cooking and processing equipment, measuring devices, knives, hand tools, and small equipment, with their uses and functions.

terms.definition: Deep, round containers used for cooking food, usually on the stovetop. Common uses: boiling, simmering, making soups, stews, or pasta.
Shallow cookware with flat bottoms, used for frying, sautéing, or baking. Common uses: frying eggs, cooking meat, sautéing vegetables.
Containers used to shape food as it cools or sets. Common uses: making cakes, gelatin desserts, chocolates, or puddings. They come in many shapes and sizes, made of metal, silicone, or plastic.
A large, deep, straight-sided pot for preparing stocks and simmering large quantities of liquids. Sizes: 8–200 quarts (liters).
A round pot of medium depth. Similar to a stockpot but shallower, making stirring or mixing easier. Used for soups, sauces, and other liquids. Sizes: 6–60 quarts (liters).
A round, broad, shallow, heavy-duty pot with straight sides. Also called a rondeau. Used for browning, braising, and stewing meats. Sizes: 11–30 quarts (liters).

Similar to a small, shallow, light saucepot, but with one long handle instead of two loop handles. May have straight or slanted sides. Used for general range top cooking. Sizes: 1 1/2–15 quarts (liters).
A straight-sided sauté pan. Similar to a shallow, straight-sided saucepan, but heavier. Used for browning, sautéing, and frying; its wide surface allows quick reduction of sauces and liquids.

A slope-sided sauté pan. Used for general sautéing and frying of meats, fish, vegetables, and eggs. The sloping sides allow the cook to flip and toss items without using a spatula, and they make it easier to get at the food when a spatula is used. Sizes: 6–14 inches (160–360 mm) top diameter.
Very heavy, thick-bottomed fry pan. Used for pan-frying when steady, even heat is desired.

A pot with two sections. The lower section, similar to a stockpot, holds boiling water. The upper section holds foods that must be cooked at low temperatures and cannot be cooked over direct heat. Size of top section: 4–36 quarts (liters).
A shallow, rectangular pan (1 inch/25 mm deep) for baking cakes, rolls, and cookies, and for baking or broiling certain meats and fish. Sizes: full pan, 18 × 26 inches (46 × 66 cm).
A rectangular pan about 2 inches (50 mm) deep. Used for general baking. Available in a variety of sizes.
A large rectangular pan, deeper and heavier than a bake pan. Used for roasting meats and poultry.

Also called counter pan, steam table pan, or service pan. A rectangular pan, usually made of stainless steel. Designed to hold foods in service counters. Also used for baking, steaming, and subsequent serving, and for storage. Standard size: 12 × 20 inches.

A round-bottomed steel pan with two loop handles. Used for stir-frying, especially in Chinese cuisine. These are best used with special burner units that have a high heat output and a broad ring-shaped support that holds it steady during cooking.
Containers placed over or inside hot water to gently heat or keep food warm without direct heat. They are commonly used for sauces, soups, and delicate foods to prevent burning or overheating.

The range is still the most important piece of cooking equipment in the kitchen. These are the top surface of a stove where burners are located, used for cooking food in pots and pans over direct heat.
Also called the commercial range. The most commonly used in professional kitchens. It provides strong heat, instant control, and is ideal for sautéing, boiling, and general cooking.
Uses coils or a smooth glass surface. It is easy to operate and clean but heats and cools more slowly than gas.
Popular in modern kitchens. It heats quickly, is energy-efficient, and safer because it only heats the cookware, not the surface.

Also called heavy-duty electric. Known for durability and consistent heat. Often used in kitchens that need long cooking hours, though it heats slower. Its disadvantage is that only iron or steel pots can be used.
Also called the flat top range. A large flat surface with a central heat source. Chefs can cook multiple dishes at different temperatures by moving pots around.
Enclosed spaces in which food is heated, usually by hot air or, in some newer kinds, by microwaves or infrared radiation.

The traditional type found in most kitchens. It operates simply by heating air in an enclosed space, using top and bottom heating elements to cook food without a fan, so heat moves naturally inside. The most common are part of the range unit, although separate units or those forming part of a broiler unit are also available.

Contains fans that circulate the air and distribute the heat rapidly throughout the interior. Because of the forced air, foods cook more quickly at lower temperatures. Also, shelves can be placed closer together than in conventional ovens without blocking the heat flow.

Kitchen equipment that cooks food using very high heat from above; sometimes called overhead to avoid confusing them with grills. Commonly used for grilling, browning, and finishing dishes like steaks, casseroles, and melted toppings.

Used for the same cooking operations as broilers, except the heat source is below the grid that holds the food rather than above it. Many people like foods cooked this way because of their charcoal taste, which is created by smoke from meat fats that drip into the heat source.

Small broilers used primarily for browning or glazing the tops of some items. A type of overhead broiler mounted on the wall or above a range. Used for quick finishing, melting cheese, glazing, and browning dishes before serving.
Flat smooth, heated surfaces on which food is cooked directly. Pancakes, French toast, hamburgers and other meats, eggs, and potato items are the foods most frequently cooked on these.

Cook meats and other foods by turning them slowly in front of electric- or gas-powered heating elements. Although especially suitable for chicken and other poultry, they can be used to cook any meat or other food that can be held on a spit or in any of various attachments or accessories. Both enclosed (oven like) and open or unclosed units are available.
Vertical ones are important and versatile tools for many kinds of food mixing and processing jobs, both in the bakeshop and in the kitchen.
A flat blade used for general mixing.
Also called the dough hook. Used for mixing and kneading yeast doughs.
Also called the rotation chopper and familiarly known as the buffalo chopper. A common piece of equipment used for general food chopping.

terms.term: The 3 Ways Measurements are Determined in a Professional Kitchen

terms.definition: Portion ones are used for measuring ingredients as well as for portioning products for service. Traditional portion ones are spring-operated and usually have a dial to indicate weight.
Those used for liquids have lips for easy pouring. Sizes are pints, quarts, half-gallons, and gallons. Each size is marked off into fourths by ridges on the sides.
Available in 1-, 1/2-, 1/3-, and 1/4-cup sizes. They can be used for both liquid and dry measures.
Used for measuring very small volumes: 1 tablespoon, 1 teaspoon, 1/2 teaspoon, and 1/4 teaspoon. They are used most often for spices and seasonings.
Measure temperature. There are many kinds for many purposes.
Indicates internal temperature of meats. It is inserted before cooking and left in the product during cooking.
Tests temperatures of frying fats.
Tests temperatures of sugar syrups.
One of the chef's most important hand tools.
The most widely available material for knife blades.
Used for knife handles. Easy to maintain, but they can become a hazard if the material is gouged, cracked, or melted.
Most frequently used knife in the kitchen, for general-purpose chopping, slicing, dicing, and so on. The blade is wide at the heel and tapers to a point.
Also called the Japanese cook's knife. A wide-bladed knife that is often used as a substitute for the traditional chef's knife. Blades are usually 5 inches (13 cm) or 7 inches (18 cm) long.
Also called the salad knife. A narrow, pointed knife 6–8 inches (160–200 mm) long. Used mostly for pantry work.
A small, pointed blade 2–4 inches (50–100 mm) long. Used for trimming and paring vegetables and fruits.
A thin, pointed blade about 6 inches (160 mm) long. Used for boning raw meats and poultry. Stiff blades are used for heavier work. Flexible blades are used for lighter work and for filleting fish.
A long, slender, flexible blade up to 14 inches (360 mm) long. Used for carving and slicing cooked meats.
Like a slicer, but with a serrated edge. Used for cutting breads, cakes, and similar items.
A heavy, broad, slightly curved blade. Used for cutting, sectioning, and trimming raw meats in the butcher shop.
Also called the steak knife. A curved, pointed blade. Used for accurate cutting of steaks.
A heavy, broad blade. Used for cutting through bones.
A short tool with a slotted, swiveling blade. Used for peeling vegetables and fruits.
Not a knife, but an essential part of the knife kit. Used for truing and maintaining knife edges.

An important partner to the knife. Hardwood ones are favored by many chefs. Hard rubber or plastic ones are thought to be more sanitary, but there is some evidence that bacteria actually survive longer on plastic and rubber than on wood. They must be kept very clean and sanitized regularly. Color-coded composite ones are designed to help reduce cross-contamination, as each color is used for a different category of food.
A heavy, two-pronged fork with a long handle. Used for lifting and turning meats and other items. Must be strong enough to hold heavy loads.
Also called the palette knife. A long, flexible blade with a rounded end. Used mostly for spreading icing on cakes and for mixing and bowl scraping.

A broad blade, bent to keep the hand off hot surfaces. Used for turning and lifting eggs, pancakes, and meats on griddles, grills, sheet pans, and so on. Also used as a scraper to clean benches and griddles.
Also called the scraper. A broad, flexible rubber or plastic tip on a long handle. Used to scrape bowls and pans. Also used for folding in egg foams and whipped cream.
A wedge-shaped offset spatula. Used for lifting pie wedges from pan.
Also called the dough knife. A broad, stiff piece of metal with a wooden handle on one edge. Used to cut pieces of dough and to scrape workbenches.
Also called the wheel knife. A round, rotating blade on a handle. Used for cutting rolled out doughs and pastry and baked pizza.
A perforated disk, slightly cupped, on a long handle. Used for skimming froth from liquids and for removing solid pieces from soups, stocks, and other liquids.
Spring-type or scissors-type tools used to pick up and handle foods.
A mixing attachment used for such tasks as beating cream and eggs and making mayonnaise. Loops of stainless-steel wire fastened to a handle.
Straight, stiff, and have relatively few wires. Used for general mixing, stirring, and beating, especially heavy liquids.
Also called piano-wire whips. They have many flexible wires. Used for whipping eggs, cream, and hollandaise, and for mixing thinner liquids.
A cone-shaped strainer. Used for straining stocks, soups, sauces, and other liquids. Pointed shape allows the cook to drain liquids through a relatively small opening.
A round-bottomed, cup-shaped tool made of screen type mesh or perforated metal. Used for straining pasta, vegetables, and so on.
Also called the tamis. A screen-type mesh supported in a round metal frame. Used for sifting flour and other dry ingredients and for puréeing soft foods.
A large, perforated bowl made of stainless steel or aluminum. Used to drain washed or cooked vegetables, salad greens, pasta, and other foods.
A tool with a hand-turned blade that forces foods through a perforated disk. Interchangeable disks produce varying degrees of coarseness or fineness. Used for puréeing foods.
A four-sided metal box with grids of varying sizes. Used for shredding and grating vegetables, cheese, citrus rinds, and other foods.
A small hand tool used for removing the colored part of citrus peels in thin strips.

A round-bottomed bowl. Used for mixing, whipping, and producing hollandaise, mayonnaise, whipped cream, and egg white foams. Round construction enables whip to reach all areas. Available in many sizes.

A manual slicing implement consisting of blades fitted in a flat metal or wood framework. A traditional one has a flat blade and a serrated blade. Additional blades can be used in combination with the flat blade to cut julienne and bâtonnet.

Cone-shaped cloths or plastic bags with an open end that can be fitted with metal tubes or tips of various shapes and sizes. Used for shaping and decorating with items such as cake icing, whipped cream, potatoes, and soft dough. Disposable plastic ones are the most sanitary.
Used to brush items with egg wash, glaze, etc.

A type of large, deep-bowled serving spoon with a long handle, designed for scooping and serving liquids like soups, stews, sauces, or gravy. Primarily a kitchen tool, they are often crafted from stainless steel, plastic, or wood.

sets.name: History of Culinary Arts
sets.description: The historical development of culinary arts, the three classes of French cuisine, the culinary timeline, and the famous French chefs and their contributions.

terms.definition: The city where the first cooking school was traced back to in the 1800s, teaching the art of American cooking along with preparing the students to pass on their knowledge to others.
In 1946, he held regular cooking classes on the art of American cooking.
She introduced French cuisine to Americans in the 1960's.

Translates to "high cuisine" and refers to a shift in French cooking from an emphasis on abundance and quantity to an emphasis on moderation and quality. Marie Antoine Careme is often credited with leading this charge in the 1800s. He taught French cooking as a sophisticated art form that required technique and precision, and his recipe manuals made French cuisine more accessible.

French cooking continued to evolve and in the 1900s, this style was born. It was defined by the use of high-quality, fresh ingredients; lighter meals; and simpler yet breathtaking presentations. Modern French cooking draws inspiration from the many cooking concepts French chefs have developed and presented over centuries.
Its rich tapestry is as ancient as the French regions themselves. Historically, local cooking was shaped by geographical conditions, available resources, and neighboring influences.
In 1500, these were established to dictate and monopolize food preparation.

A Parisian who in 1765 opened the first free-standing restaurant in Paris. He was challenged in court by the guild of stew makers because he served sheep's feet in a cream sauce; the guild claimed he replicated the dish, but he won the case by claiming that he didn't stew the feet in the sauce but served it with the sauce. Because of what happened, he unintentionally changed the development of food-service history.
The name Boulanger gave his 1765 Parisian establishment, from a word literally meaning "fortifying" or to bring life back.
1789-1799. The social structure in France changed. The aristocracy guilds and their monopolies were abolished. The growing restaurant industry emerged.

1783-1833. Also known as "The King of Chefs and the Chef of Kings" and the First Celebrity Chef. His goal was to achieve "lightness, grace, order and perspicuity in the preparation and presentation of food." As a saucier, he standardized the use of roux and devised a system to classify sauces; as a culinary professional, he designed kitchen tools, equipment and uniforms. An illustrious French chef, the most famous author and culinary artist during the 1800s and one of the most prolific food writers of the 19th century, he codified the four original Mother Sauces in the early 1800s.

1846-1935. Known as the "Father of Modern French Culinary Arts," the "king of the Kitchen" and the "father of modern French cuisine." He simplified food preparation and dining and conceptualized the so-called kitchen brigade system used in large kitchens today. He classified the five families of sauces. An author of par excellence, he wrote Le livres de menus, Ma Cuisine, and Le Guide Culinaire, which is still in use today. He recognized garde manger as a specialized type of work significantly different from hot foods cooking, and physically separated the garde manger station from the hot line.
1886-1899. The "First renowned chef of an American restaurant," Delmonico in New York City. His goal was to serve fresh seasonal and locally grown produce.
1897-1955. He refined and modernized classical cuisine and laid the groundwork for Nouvelle Cuisine.

1920-2009. The "Father of modern French Pastry." He began a culinary school named Le Ecole Norte. He developed innovations in modern pastries such as Bavarians, Charlottes and Mousses, and mastered the techniques in freezing baked products.

Founder of modern French cuisine. He is credited as the first to use roux as a thickener, and to refine the technique of dough lamination to make an early form of puff pastry. He lightened and simplified European cooking and popularized fresh vegetables and herbs.

sets.name: Sanitation and Food Safety in Culinary Operations
sets.description: Food safety, R.A. 10611, the three major types of food hazards, food handlers, and the ten principles of safe food handling and hygiene.

terms.definition: Refers to handling, preparing and storing food in a way to best reduce the risk of individuals becoming sick from foodborne illnesses. It is the protection of consumer health and well-being by safeguarding food from anything that can cause harm.

An act to strengthen the food safety regulatory system in the country to protect consumer health and facilitate market access of local foods and food products, and for other purposes; otherwise known as the "Food Safety Act of 2013".

terms.term: The 3 Major Types of Hazards in Food Safety

terms.definition: Foodborne organisms such as bacteria, molds, viruses, and parasites. Their seriousness varies greatly. It is difficult to avoid microorganisms because they are everywhere. However, most are inactivated or killed by adequate cooking and/or their numbers are kept to a minimum by sufficient cooling.

Any substances hazardous to health. These can come from plants (herbs, fruit pits, and mushrooms), animals (fish), or those used in agriculture or industry that may end up in food unintentionally. Both fish and shellfish may harbor toxins causing foodborne illness. Not all fishes and plants are consumable, such as pufferfish and amanita species of mushrooms.
During this time there is an increased chance that seafood products are poisonous, which is why people are very careful in buying seafood products.

Items in food and beverages that can harm the consumer's health, including glass (the most common), bone, metal, wood, stones, false fingernails, toothpicks, watches, jewelry, insects, staples from food boxes, and many other foreign items that have been known to find their way into the food supply.
Those whose work involves food; anyone who works in a food business where food is processed, prepared, stored, distributed, or exposed for sale. Their role is ensuring the food produced is safe.

terms.term: The 10 Principles of Safe Food Handling & Hygiene
answers: Cooking by appropriate temperature and procedures

sets.name: The Kitchen Organization and Layout
sets.description: The kitchen and its areas and sub-sections, the work triangle and layout shapes, and the classical kitchen brigade positions.

terms.definition: The heart of food operations where preparation, cooking, and storage happen. A room or part of a room used for cooking and food preparation, with a stove, sink with hot and cold running water, a refrigerator and cabinets arranged there. Its main function is cooking or preparing food but it may also be used for dining, food storage, entertaining, and dishwashing.

terms.term: The 4 Other Functions of the Kitchen
answers: Production and presentation of quality foods using a tested recipe
Preparation of menus and standard recipe
Dispatching of food to foodcounters and dining areas

terms.definition: Where ingredients are washed, cut, and made ready for cooking.
Where food is actually cooked using different methods such as boiling, frying, or baking.
Where food is arranged and prepared for serving.
Where ingredients and supplies are kept (dry, cold, and frozen).
Where used tools, utensils, and equipment are cleaned and sanitized.
Part of the kitchen where dishes like salads and cold cuts, etc. are prepared. It is also in this area that cold and fruit platters as well as garnishes are prepared.
Part of the kitchen that handles the preparation of all hot items like stocks, soup, sauces and main dishes.
The area where meat, chicken and seafood items are portion cut in accordance with standard sizes and weights that are indicated in standard recipes.
The section responsible for preparing all baked goods, desserts, and other sweet treats. This area typically includes a variety of specialized equipment and tasks, such as baking and decorating.

One of the most basic layout principles. An imaginary line drawn from each of the three primary work stations in the kitchen — the food storage, preparation/cooking, and clean-up stations. By drawing these lines, you can assess the distance required to move to and from each area and thus determine how well the traffic will flow. To help avoid traffic flow problems, it should have a perimeter that measures less than 26 feet.

terms.term: The 3 Primary Kitchen Work Stations Constituting the Work Triangle

terms.definition: The refrigerator and pantry are the major items here. Cabinetries like lazy Susan or swing-out pantry units add function and convenience. Options like wine racks, spice racks, and roll-out trays help to organize your groceries.
The range, oven, microwave, and smaller appliances are found in this area. Counter space is important in this section.

Everyone's least favorite activity is one of the kitchen's most important. This area is home to the sink, waste disposal, and dishwasher. Cabinetry for this station is designed to organize the trash bin cabinet and roll-out tray baskets for storage convenience.

Named for the shape it resembles, this kitchen is popular in large and small homes alike. Perfect for families who use their kitchens a great deal; provides plenty of counter space; efficient work triangle.

This kitchen shape is one of the most flexible and most popular, providing a compact triangle. Very flexible layout design; major appliances can be placed in a variety of areas; work areas are close to each other.

Extremely popular in homes today and most often seen in L-Shaped kitchens. It can not only keep work areas traffic-free, but also create a wealth of extra counter and storage space. It can be an indispensable food preparation station or act as a butcher block area.

Built very much like the U-Shaped with the addition of an elongated partial wall, it offers a great deal of space. Plenty of counter and cabinet space; multiple cooks can function well in this layout; can convert one cabinet leg into a breakfast bar or entertaining area.

Also called the corridor kitchen. This style makes the most out of a smaller space. Great for smaller kitchens; appliances are close to one another; easy for one cook to maneuver; can easily convert to a U-Shape by closing off one end.

Refers to the structured arrangement of roles and responsibilities of staff, ensuring that each member has a specific duty to maintain efficiency, coordination, and smooth workflow in food preparation.
The most common system used in kitchen organization, developed by Auguste Escoffier.

Is responsible for overall management of kitchen; supervises staff, creates menus and new recipes with the assistance of the restaurant manager, makes purchases of raw food items, trains apprentices, and maintains a sanitary and hygienic environment for the preparation of food.

Sub Chief or Deputy Kitchen Chef. He or she receives orders directly from the Chef de Cuisine for the management of the kitchen. Often serves as the representative when the Chef de Cuisine is not present.
Station Head. He or she is responsible for managing a given station in the kitchen, specializing in preparing particular dishes there.
Assistant Station Chef. Does most of the actual preparation of food in the kitchen.
Junior Cook. Also works in a specific station, but reports directly to the Chef de Partie and takes care of the tools for the station.
Sauté Station Chef. Prepares sauces and warm hors d'oeuvres, completes meat dishes, and in smaller restaurants, may work on fish dishes and prepare sautéed items.
Roast Station Chef. He or she is responsible for the preparation of all roasted food items. Manages a team of cooks that roasts and broils.
Grill Station Chef. Prepares grilled foods instead of the rôtisseur. He or she is responsible for all the grilled foods.
Fish Station Chef. He or she is responsible for the preparation of all fish and seafood dishes.
Fry Station Chef. He or she is responsible for fried meats, seafood and etc. In larger kitchens, prepares fried foods instead of the rôtisseur.
Chicken Station Chef. He or she is responsible for the preparation of all chicken or poultry dishes.
Vegetable Station Chef. Prepares the vegetable dishes and also reports to the entremetier.
Soup Station Chef. Prepares the soups and reports to the entremetier.

terms.term: The 4 Specialized Areas of the Kitchen Brigade

terms.definition: Petit Fours Preparer. In larger restaurants, prepares candies and petits fours instead of the pâtissier.
Frozen and Cold Dessert Preparer. In larger restaurants, prepares frozen and cold desserts instead of the pâtissier. Makes ice carving for presentation.
Cake Designer or Decorator. In larger restaurants, prepares show pieces and specialty cakes instead of the pâtissier.
Baker. In larger restaurants, prepares bread, cakes, and breakfast pastries instead of the pâtissier.
Butcher. Butchers meats, poultry, and sometimes fish; may also be in charge of breading meat and fish items.
Announcer or Expediter. Takes orders from the dining room and distributes them to the various stations; may also be performed by the sous-chef de partie.
Spare Hand or Rounds Man. He or she moves throughout the kitchen, assisting other positions in kitchen.
Literally "Kitchen Boy". In larger restaurants, performs preparatory and "auxiliary" (back-up) work for support.
Apprentice. Students gaining theoretical and practical training in the kitchen.
Dishwasher or Kitchen Porter. Cleans dishes and utensils.
Pot and Pan Washer. Takes care of all the pots and pans instead of the plongeur.

sets.name: Functions and Binary Numbers
sets.description: Functions (domain, range, and their operations) and binary numbers.

terms.definition: An equation that relates or connects one set into other sets, transforming an input (the domain) into an output (the range).
The input of a function; the set of X values.
The output of a function; the set of Y or f(x) values.
A number that belongs to the base 2 number system, made up of only 0's and 1's; it is written with a subscript 2 to distinguish it from a decimal number.

sets.name: Mathematics in Our World
sets.description: Key terms on mathematics in nature: patterns, symmetry, the Fibonacci sequence, and the Golden Ratio.

terms.definition: An area of knowledge that includes the topics of numbers, formulas and related structures, shapes and the spaces in which they are contained, and quantities and their changes.

terms.term: Applications of Mathematics in the World
answers: Planning a market list and grocery shopping

terms.definition: Regular, repeated, recurring forms and designs; used to identify relationships and find logical connections to form generalizations.
Indicates that you can draw an imaginary line across an object and the resulting parts are mirror images of each other.
An integer in the infinite sequence that starts with 1, 1, 2, 3, 5, 8, 13, ..., of which the first two terms are 1 and 1 and each succeeding term is the sum of the two immediately preceding.
The greatest mathematician of the Middle Ages, born in 1170 and died in 1240; he introduced the Arabic number system in Europe.
A French mathematician, physicist, and astronomer, born in Rennes and died in Paris, France, in 1856; the closed-form formula expressing Fibonacci numbers is named in his honor.
The closed-form formula used to find the nth Fibonacci number.

Usually rounded off to 1.618; it appears in all forms of nature and science, including some unexpected places such as seed heads, shells, spiral galaxies, hurricanes, DNA molecules, and animal bodies.

terms.term: Places Where the Golden Ratio Appears in Nature

sets.name: Sets
sets.description: Set theory basics: ways to describe sets, kinds of sets, set relations, subsets, and set operations.

terms.definition: A well-defined collection of distinct objects, whose members or elements can be numbers, people, and other sets.
A method of describing sets in the form of a sentence.
A method in which the elements of the given set are listed or enumerated, separated by commas, inside a pair of braces.
A method in which the common characteristic of the elements is defined, using the form {x/x...}.
A set with no element.
The totality of the elements of the sets.
A set with countable elements.
A set with uncountable elements.

terms.term: 4 Relations of Two or More Sets

terms.definition: Sets that have the same elements.
Sets that have the same number of elements.
Sets that have at least one common element.
Sets that have no common element.
A set every element of which can be found on a bigger set.
A subset in which the first set is equal to the second set.
A subset in which the first set is not equal to the second set.
Used to know the number of subsets; equal to 2^n, where n is the number of elements.
The operation whose elements are found in set A or set B or in both.
The operation whose elements are found in set A and set B.
The operation whose elements are found in A but not in set B.
The operation whose elements are found in the universal set but not in set A.

sets.name: Citizenship Training
sets.description: Historical and legal basis of NSTP, key laws, the Constitution, human rights principles, and voter citizenship education from Module 1.

terms.definition: Period when a training similar to ROTC was introduced in the Philippines.
Where the real ROTC in the country started, in 1912.

Otherwise known as the "National Defense Act," passed during the presidency of President Manuel L. Quezon; NSTP started through its passage. Obligated all male citizens between the ages of 18 and 30 to military service to create an independent Philippine Army.
Post-war order stating that the ROTC Course was made mandatory for all colleges.
What ROTC was replaced by in the post-Marcos era (1980).

Otherwise known as the "National Service Law"; amended Commonwealth Act No. 1 and was signed into law by President Ferdinand E. Marcos Sr. on 8 August 1980. Made national service obligatory for all Filipino citizens (civic welfare service, law enforcement service, and military service).

Otherwise known as the "Citizen Armed Forces of the Philippines Reservist Act," signed by President Corazon Aquino to back the CMT. Provided for the organization, training, and utilization of reservists called "Citizen Soldiers."
The reservists provided for under the Act; the primary manpower for the reservist organization are graduates of the Reserve Officers' Training Corps basic and advance courses.

A 19-year-old UST Engineering student found wrapped in a carpet and floating in the Pasig River, his face wrapped in packing tape and his hands bound; reported to be a victim of illegal ROTC practices (March 18, 2001 ROTC Crisis).

The National Service Training Program Act of 2001; affirms that the prime duty of the government shall be to serve and protect its citizens, and that it is the responsibility of all citizens to defend the security of the State.
Constitutional basis stating that the prime duty of the government is to serve and protect the people.
Constitutional basis on the maintenance of peace and order, the protection of life, liberty, and property, and the promotion of the general welfare.

Constitutional basis stating that the State recognizes the vital role of the youth in nation-building and shall promote and protect their physical, moral, spiritual, intellectual, and social well-being.
A program aimed at enhancing civic consciousness and defense preparedness in the youth by developing the ethics of service and patriotism while undergoing training in any of its program components.
The grouping of students enrolled to different schools and taking up the same NSTP component into one group under the management and supervision of a designated school.
A system of enrolment where a student is officially enrolled in an academic program of an origin school but is allowed to enroll in the NSTP component of another accepting school.
The official act of calling upon ROTC Reservists and NSRC Reservists and Units to report to their respective designated mobilization centers.
Universities, colleges, and other educational institutions offering higher education.
The established organization of students or graduates of the CWTS or LTS components of the NSTP.
The established organization of students or graduates of the ROTC component of the NSTP.
All incoming freshmen students, male and female, are covered by the program.

answers: Those who completed any of the three NSTP components

terms.definition: Constitution of September 15, 1898; declared Philippine independence in Kawit, Cavite on June 12, 1898.
Date when Philippine Independence was eventually achieved.
New constitution drafted after Ferdinand Marcos manipulated an ongoing Constitutional Convention during Martial Law in 1972.
Constitution drafted in 133 days by an appointed Constitutional Commission under President Corazon Aquino and ratified by the people on February 2, 1987.

We, the sovereign Filipino people, imploring the aid of Almighty God, in order to build a just and humane society and establish a government that shall embody our ideals and aspirations, promote the common good, conserve and develop our patrimony, and secure to ourselves and our posterity the blessings of independence and democracy under the rule of law and regime of truth, justice, freedom, love, equality, and peace, do ordain and promulgate this Constitution.

Article III of the Constitution. It guarantees rights such as: due process and equal protection of the laws; security against unreasonable searches and seizures; freedom of speech, of expression, and of the press; the right to information on matters of public concern; just compensation for private property taken for public use; no criminal charge without due process of law; speedy disposition of cases; no imprisonment for debt or non-payment of a poll tax; and no double jeopardy.

terms.term: Duties and Responsibilities of Filipino Citizens
terms.definition: They include: defend our country from enemies and invaders; pay taxes willingly and promptly; be loyal to our country; take care of and conserve our natural resources; help our country grow and develop; keep our surroundings clean; study well and become a productive individual; obey the laws and maintain peace and order; preserve the Filipino culture and identity; participate actively in various government programs; vote wisely; and respect the rights of others.
Human rights principle: "All human beings are born free and equal in dignity and rights."
Human rights principle: human rights are inherent to the dignity of every human person. All human rights have equal status and cannot be positioned in a hierarchical order.
Human rights principle: the fulfillment of one right often depends, wholly or in part, upon the fulfillment of others.
Human rights principle: all individuals are equal as human beings and by virtue of the inherent dignity of each human person.
Human rights principle: all people have the right to participate in and access information relating to the decision-making processes that affect their lives and well-being.

Human rights principle: States and other duty-bearers are answerable for the observance of human rights and must comply with the legal norms and standards enshrined in international human rights instruments.

terms.term: 6 United Nations Principles on Human Rights

terms.definition: The Flag and Heraldic Code of the Philippines.

terms.term: 3 Chapters of the Flag and Heraldic Code

terms.definition: One of the foundations of democracy and one of the constitutional rights of a citizen of the Philippines; one of the ways to make your future less scary. "Every vote matters."
Held every 6 years.
Held on the second Monday of May every 3 years from 1992; also called the "Midterm Elections."
Candidate checklist (Kakayahan): capable of the task that the position requires; the candidate has the experience to handle the challenges.
Candidate checklist (Kaalaman): right educational background for the position; the candidate has the skills required and is aware of the issues that concern our communities/nation.
Candidate checklist (Kredibilidad): possesses the leadership qualities of a public servant—honest, trustworthy, and has integrity.

sets.name: Drug Education
sets.description: Concepts of drug use, drug classes, abuse vs. addiction, the national drug situation, and the youth's role in prevention.

terms.definition: The planned provision of information, guidelines, resources, and skills relevant to living in a world where psychoactive substances are widely available.
The Comprehensive Dangerous Drugs Act of 2002; mandates the government to pursue an intensive and unrelenting campaign against the trafficking and use of dangerous drugs and other similar substances.
The Dangerous Drugs Act of 1972; provides funds and regulates the importation, sale, manufacture, possession, and use of prohibited and regulated drugs.

A chemical substance that brings about physical, emotional, or behavioral change in a person taking it. Taken through ingestion, inhalation, injection, used as a suppository, or applied topically; harmful when taken in excess.
A class of drugs that speed up messages travelling between the brain and body; increases alertness and physical disposition. Examples: coffee and cocaine.
Drugs that reduce anxiety and excitement; medications that slow down brain activity, leading to relaxation, reduced anxiety, and drowsiness. Examples: alcohol and tranquilizers.
Drugs that affect sensation, thinking, self-awareness, and emotion, change time and space perception, and create delusions and hallucinations. Examples: marijuana and mescaline.
Drugs that relieve pain and often induce sleep; any psychoactive compound with numbing or paralyzing properties. Examples: opium and heroine.
Generally a drug or chemical whose manufacture, possession, or use is regulated by a government.

terms.term: 5 Reasons Why People Turn to Drugs

terms.definition: The use of a chemical substance, forbidden or not forbidden, which results in an individual's physical, mental, or social impairment; using drugs without benefit or prescription, using legal or illegal substances in ways you shouldn't, or taking more than the regular dose of pills or using someone else's prescription.

A disease that affects your brain and behavior; you can't resist the urge to use drugs no matter how much harm they may cause, and you can't stop. It makes you lose self-control and can lead to damaging behaviors, and its physical changes can last a long time.

terms.term: 4 Signs and Symptoms of Drug Abuse
answers: Change in moods depending on the drug taken
Wearing sunglasses to hide constricted pupils

terms.term: 4 Signs and Symptoms of Drug Addiction
answers: An urge to use the drug every day or many times a day
Always have the drug with you
You feel sick when you try to quit

terms.definition: The majority of drug dependents in the country — users who use several types of illegal drugs.
Methamphetamine hydrochloride; remains the most abused drug in the country, followed by marijuana or cannabis.
Well known for being a "party drug," commonly used in parties and nightlife.
A drug preference usually abused by street children.

answers: African Drug Syndicates using OFW couriers

terms.definition: Remains the main hub in the manufacturing of shabu in the country.
The region where marijuana cultivation is still largely concentrated.
