answers: Get involved in program design and implementation
Be a peer educator and advocate health-promoting behaviors
Learn to deal with life's pressures
Never give in to peer pressures
Help those your age engage in healthy activities

sets.name: Cordillera Administrative Region
sets.description: The provinces and capitals of the Cordillera Administrative Region, with its attractions, foods and delicacies, and festivals.

terms.term: 6 Provinces of the Cordillera Administrative Region and Their Capitals

terms.definition: Serves as the regional center of the Cordillera Administrative Region (CAR). Widely known as the "Summer Capital of the Philippines" because of its cool climate.
Known as the "Salad Bowl of the Philippines" due to its thriving vegetable farming industry, which supplies a large portion of the country's highland vegetables.

An attraction in Apayao. Originally constructed in 1684 and designed with fortress-like features to protect Spanish colonizers from local resistance. Abandoned in 1815 due to persistent attacks by the Isneg people, one of the major Indigenous groups in the province. Spanish authorities regained partial control of the area in 1891.

An attraction in Abra. Showcases the long-standing weaving tradition of the province, where local artisans produce textiles using traditional handlooms. Visitors can observe the weaving process and directly purchase locally made products.

An attraction in Kalinga. Widely recognized for its strong indigenous heritage and scenic mountainous surroundings. Home to Apo Whang-Od and, due to its remote location, has maintained much of its traditional lifestyle.
A National Living Treasure from Buscalan Village, Kalinga, known for preserving the traditional tattooing practice called "batok."
The traditional tattooing practice preserved in Buscalan Village, which symbolizes bravery, identity, and social status in Kalinga culture.

An attraction in Kalinga. Famous for its natural ridge formation that resembles a sleeping woman, which is why it is widely known as the "Sleeping Beauty Mountain." One of Kalinga's most recognizable natural landmarks and a contributor to its growing ecotourism industry.

An attraction in Mountain Province. An ancient burial tradition practiced by the Igorot people, where coffins are placed along limestone cliffs to bring the deceased closer to ancestral spirits. Traditionally, elderly individuals prepare their own coffins, while families assist when necessary.

terms.term: 6 Aspects of the Kankanaey Burial Tradition

terms.definition: Only full-blooded Kankanaey elders who are married, have children and grandchildren, and die peacefully of old age may receive a cliffside burial.
Elders traditionally carve their own pine-log coffin while still alive. The coffin is made short because the body will be placed in a fetal position.
The deceased is placed on a death chair for a 3-6 day vigil. The body is preserved through continuous smoking, while a village elder performs rituals and animal offerings.
The body is wrapped in a traditional woven cloth and placed in a fetal position, symbolizing a return to the beginning of life.
Young men carry the body to the cliff, believing this passes on the deceased's wisdom and blessings. Skilled climbers then secure the coffin to the cliff face.
The Kankanaey believe elevated coffins bring the soul closer to heaven and ancestors, while also protecting the remains from animals and natural hazards and preserving farmland.

An attraction in Mountain Province. A traditional stone structure used as a communal gathering place where elders discuss social, political, and spiritual concerns. A central fireplace symbolizes unity, while wooden poles are used to display animal offerings during rituals. It reflects the indigenous governance system and the importance of collective decision-making in Cordilleran society.

An attraction in Ifugao. Among the most iconic cultural landscapes in the country, believed to have been carved by the Ifugao people more than 2,000 years ago. They demonstrate advanced indigenous knowledge in engineering and sustainable agriculture, and are often referred to as the "Eighth Wonder of the World."

An attraction in Benguet. An open-air museum and art colony designed to replicate a traditional Cordilleran village, featuring reconstructed, authentic Ifugao and Kalinga huts. It functions as a living museum where visitors can learn about indigenous lifestyles, artistic traditions, and local craftsmanship.

An attraction in Benguet. One of the most recognized religious landmarks in the region and one of the largest churches in Baguio City. During World War II, it served as an evacuation center for civilians, and it was the only major building to survive the devastating carpet-bombing of Baguio City during the war. Its elevated location provides a panoramic view of the city.

An attraction in Benguet. The official summer residence of the President of the Philippines. Originally built in 1908 as the residence of American Governor-Generals, and declared a National Historic Landmark in 2009. It has reopened to the public as the Presidential Museum after a major renovation.

Deeply shaped by the region's agricultural traditions and cool mountain climate, commonly using locally available ingredients such as rice, root crops, pork, and native plants. Traditional cooking methods often include fermenting, curing, and smoking meat to help preserve it for longer periods. Because the Cordillera is a mountainous inland region with limited access to seafood, communities relied more on livestock such as pigs and chickens.

A delicacy of Apayao. A traditional chicken dish prepared through a cultural ritual process, cooked with native spices and known for its smoky flavor. The chicken is traditionally tapped gently before slaughter, a practice believed to improve the flavor and performed as part of indigenous rituals.

A delicacy of Abra. A noodle dish made with fresh egg noodles, pork, and vegetables, known for its rich broth and savory flavor. The noodles are traditionally handmade, giving them a firm and chewy texture.

A delicacy of Kalinga. A traditional rice cake (kakanin) made from glutinous rice and local ingredients, commonly prepared during gatherings, festivals, and cultural celebrations. It is served as a dessert because it is coated with a rich, sweet coconut caramel (latik or ladok) made from coconut cream and sugar. Its name comes from its shape, as the sticky rice dough is molded to resemble a human tongue before being wrapped and cooked.

A delicacy of Mountain Province. A traditional rice wine made from fermented glutinous rice through a natural fermentation process using indigenous yeast. It is an essential part of indigenous ceremonies such as weddings, thanksgiving rituals, harvest festivals, and peace agreements. Offering it to guests is a sign of hospitality, friendship, and respect.

A delicacy of Ifugao. A traditional blood sausage made from pork blood, meat, fat, and local spices, known for its rich, savory flavor. Commonly served as a viand, usually grilled, boiled, or fried and eaten with rice. It reflects the Ifugao tradition of using every part of the pig.

A delicacy of Benguet. A traditional preserved pork cured with salt and smoked or sun-dried, developed as a meat preservation method before refrigeration. Commonly used as a seasoning to add a smoky, salty, and savory flavor to soups and vegetable dishes, and can also be grilled or cooked and served as a viand. An important ingredient in Cordilleran dishes such as Pinikpikan.

terms.term: 6 Foods and Delicacies of the Cordillera Administrative Region

terms.definition: A festival of Abra. Held March 6-9 to recognize bamboo as one of the province's most important natural resources. It coincides with the founding anniversary of Abra and features street dancing, bamboo craft exhibits, and cultural presentations, highlighting the creativity and skills of local artisans who use bamboo in various products and industries.

A festival of Apayao. Held February 1-14, with the main celebration on February 14, as both a thanksgiving ritual for a bountiful harvest and a commemoration of the province's founding anniversary. It honors the traditions of the Isneg people through cultural performances, music, and dances.

A festival of Benguet. Held in February in Baguio City and widely known as the Flower Festival due to its colorful floral floats and street dancing performances. It began as a symbol of recovery after the 1990 Luzon earthquake and has grown into a major tourism event.

A festival of Ifugao. Held in April, usually every 3 years in Banaue, as a cultural thanksgiving event that highlights the traditions of the Ifugao people. It features indigenous games, rituals, and cultural performances that showcase community values and identity.

A festival of Kalinga. Held February 13-16 to preserve and promote the rich cultural traditions of the Kalinga people. It is inspired by the ullalim epic chant, which tells stories of heroism, ancestry, and community values, and includes indigenous dances, rituals, and performances.

A festival of Mountain Province. Held the first week of April as part of the founding anniversary of the province. The word "lang-ay" means unity and sharing, reflecting cooperation among indigenous communities. It promotes appreciation of cultural heritage while supporting tourism and local economic development.

terms.term: 6 Festivals of the Cordillera Administrative Region

sets.name: Introduction to Tourism
sets.description: The 4As of Tourism and basic facts about the Philippines: islands, landforms, UNESCO sites, climate, language, and ethnic groups.

terms.definition: A basic framework used to analyze the elements that make a destination appealing to visitors.
Refers to how easily tourists can reach and move within a destination.
The main reasons tourists visit a destination. A mix of these makes a place more appealing to a wider range of visitors.
Provides tourists with a place to stay, rest, and access basic services.

terms.term: 4 Accommodation Options (vary depending on budget and preference)

terms.definition: The experiences that tourists engage in during their visit.

terms.term: Importance of Well-Planned and Diverse Activities
terms.definition: They make destinations more enjoyable and memorable; offering a variety of activities helps attract different types of tourists and encourages longer stays.
The largest island, located in the northern region of the archipelago; also the most populous island. It has a land area of approximately 40,420 square miles.
The Philippines is consisting of thousand islands and there is no known smallest island using any known technological definition and due to high tide and low tide variation.

terms.term: Number of Islands in the Philippines
terms.definition: The Philippines has 7,641 islands.

The total coastline length ranges from 17,500 kilometers (Philippine figure) to 36,289 kilometers (U.S. figure). According to Philippines Tourism USA, the Philippines has the fourth largest coastline in the world.
The highest point in the Philippines. It has an elevation of 9,692 feet and is located in between Davao City and Davao Del Sur.

Originally called Minuro; considered as the oldest province in the Philippines. Established during the 12th Century and became an independent province when President Magsaysay signed it into law on April 25, 1956.
As of 2024, the Philippines has 82 provinces, distributed across the three major island groups.

terms.term: Distribution of the 82 Provinces Across the 3 Major Island Groups

terms.definition: The Philippines is divided into 18 administrative regions.

terms.term: The 18 Administrative Regions of the Philippines, by name
answers: Bangsamoro Autonomous Region in Muslim Mindanao

terms.definition: The largest province in terms of land area. It has a land area of 17,030.75 sq. kms.
Known as the smallest province. It has a land area of 202.33 sq. kms.

terms.term: 6 UNESCO World Heritage Sites in the Philippines
answers: Rice Terraces of the Philippine Cordilleras

terms.definition: The Philippines has a tropical climate characterized by warm temperatures, high humidity, and significant rainfall throughout the year.
Filipino and English are the official languages used and taught in the Philippines.

terms.term: Number of Ethnic Groups in the Philippines
terms.definition: There are more than 100 ethnic groups in the Philippines.

terms.term: 6 Most Dominant Ethnic Groups in the Philippines

sets.name: Region 1 - Ilocos Region
sets.description: The provinces and capitals of the Ilocos Region, with its attractions, foods and delicacies, and festivals.

terms.term: 4 Provinces of Region 1 and Their Capitals

terms.definition: An attraction in Ilocos Norte. Also known as San Agustin Church. Recognized as a UNESCO World Heritage Site. Characterized by thick coral stone walls and massive buttresses designed to withstand earthquakes.
An attraction in Ilocos Norte. A centuries-old bell tower that mysteriously sinks into the ground over time; a symbol of Laoag's Spanish colonial past.

An attraction in Ilocos Sur. A well-preserved heritage street in Vigan City. Features cobblestone roads lined with Spanish-era houses. Showcases antique lamps and traditional horse-drawn carriages (kalesa).

An attraction in Ilocos Sur. A historic bell tower located on a hill overlooking Vigan. Part of the Saint Augustine Parish Church complex. Built in the 16th century and used as a watchtower during the Spanish colonial period.
An attraction in Ilocos Sur. A popular tourist attraction in Vigan City. Owned by former governor Luis "Chavit" Singson. Features a mini zoo, safari gallery, and recreational facilities.
An attraction in La Union. One of the well-known natural attractions in La Union. Located in San Gabriel and accessible through trekking. Popular for swimming and nature trips.

An attraction in La Union. One of the largest Taoist temples in the Philippines, and the first Taoist temple in the Philippines. Built in 1975 with the support of the local Chinese-Filipino community.
An attraction in Pangasinan. Located in Alaminos City. Composed of 124 islands scattered across Lingayen Gulf. Known for island hopping, snorkeling, and beach activities.

An attraction in Pangasinan. A major Roman Catholic pilgrimage site. Officially known as the Minor Basilica of Our Lady of the Rosary of Manaoag. Devotees visit the shrine due to its long-standing religious significance.
A delicacy of Ilocos Norte. A savory pastry filled with vegetables, egg, and Ilocos longganisa. Known for its crispy orange-colored rice flour crust.
A delicacy of Ilocos Sur. A garlicky and slightly sour native sausage. Commonly served with garlic rice and fried egg.
A delicacy of La Union. A fresh salad of live shrimp mixed with vinegar, onions, and tomatoes.
A delicacy of Pangasinan. A soft, bite-sized steamed rice cake. Made from rice flour, sugar, and yeast, known for its slightly sticky texture.

terms.term: 4 Foods and Delicacies of the Ilocos Region

terms.definition: A festival of Ilocos Norte. Held every February 10 in Laoag City, this festival honors St. William the Hermit through street dancing, cultural performances, and religious activities. Its name is inspired by a traditional Ilocano folk song which reflects the rich heritage of the community.

A festival of Ilocos Sur. Celebrated every January 22 in Vigan City, this festival showcases the famous Ilocos longganisa through lively street dancing and fun eating contests that highlight the region's well-loved delicacy.

A social event of La Union. Surfers from all around the world come together for this yearly event every January. It supports local tourism and businesses while promoting La Union as a premier surfing destination.

A festival of Pangasinan. Held each year in Dagupan City from April 16 to May 4 to commemorate the abundance of milkfish production in the area. It includes dancing, fluvial processions, and grill parties attracting lots of tourists.

terms.term: 4 Festivals and Social Events of the Ilocos Region

sets.name: Region 2 - Cagayan Valley Region
sets.description: The provinces and capitals of the Cagayan Valley Region, with its attractions, foods and delicacies, and festivals.

terms.term: 5 Provinces of Region 2 and Their Capitals

terms.definition: An attraction in Batanes. Also known as Tukon Church, located on a hillside in Basco near Mt. Iraya. Built by local craftsmen using traditional Ivatan architectural design. Reflects Ivatan culture and adaptation to the natural environment.

An attraction in Batanes. Considered the oldest surviving traditional Ivatan house in Batanes. Located in Batan Island and made of stone and lime with a cogon roof. Originally built in 1917 and maintained through periodic roof replacement.
An attraction in Batanes. An unmanned shop located in Ivana, Batanes. Operates on a self-service system based on honesty and trust. Customers record purchases and leave payment in a designated box.

An attraction in Cagayan. An 11-hectare site featuring life-sized Stations of the Cross. A known pilgrimage destination, especially during Holy Week. Offers a scenic view of the Cagayan River and is accessible by stairs.

An attraction in Cagayan. Located in the Peñablanca Protected Landscape and Seascape. One of over 300 caves in the area, with seven chambers open to visitors. Features a natural chapel inside one of its chambers.

An attraction in Isabela. Known for its red brick façade and detailed architectural design. Completed in 1805 under Dominican missionaries. Features a rare cylindrical bell tower and is recognized as a heritage structure.
An attraction in Isabela. A large representation of the traditional reclining chair (butaka). Made from materials such as narra wood and woven rattan. Symbolizes local craftsmanship in Ilagan City.
An attraction in Nueva Vizcaya. Features Baroque-inspired architectural elements. Functions as the seat of the Diocese of Bayombong.

An attraction in Nueva Vizcaya. Located within Mount Pulag National Park in the Cordillera Mountain Range. The highest peak in Luzon at 2,922 meters above sea level. Known for its "sea of clouds" and diverse flora and fauna.

An attraction in Quirino. A scenic section of the Cagayan River with limestone formations. Known for activities such as rafting, kayaking, and swimming. Considered a major ecotourism attraction in the province.
An attraction in Quirino. A designated outdoor area for motocross and trail riding. Serves as the main venue for the Quirino Motorismo Festival. Attracts riders from different parts of the country.
A food and delicacy of Batanes. A traditional Ivatan dish made from grated banana pith and fish or meat. Known for its simple preparation and use of locally available ingredients.
A food and delicacy of Cagayan. A noodle dish topped with sautéed meat, vegetables, and egg. Served with a savory broth and known for its rich flavor.
A food and delicacy of Isabela. A roasted sheep dish known for its tender meat and crispy skin. Often served during special occasions and local celebrations.
A food and delicacy of Nueva Vizcaya. Exotic food option in the province.
A food and delicacy of Quirino. A savory dish made from pork, liver, and vegetables cooked in vinegar and soy sauce. Known for its slightly tangy and salty flavor.

A festival of Batanes. Celebrated every June 19 to July 1, this festival highlights the unique practice of the Ivatan people of voluntary work and a strong sense of community. Bayanihan is given emphasis during this event. The celebration includes cultural presentations, local games, and social gatherings.

A festival of Cagayan. Held every May 10, this festival is a thanksgiving celebration in honor of St. Peter Thelmo, the patron saint of the community. It is widely known for its boat races along the Cagayan River, participated in by local fishermen.

A festival of Isabela. Celebrated every April 20 to 23, this festival is a thanksgiving for the city's agricultural abundance. It highlights the contributions of farmers to the community and showcases the cultural diversity within the province.

A festival of Nueva Vizcaya. Held annually every August, this festival celebrates the bountiful harvest of citrus fruits in the province. The event features agricultural exhibits, fruit displays, and competitions. It aims to promote local produce, support farmers, and boost tourism.

A festival of Quirino. Known as the only motorbiking festival in the country, this event is held every July. Participants from different parts of the country join motocross races, trail rides, and other riding activities. It also highlights the natural terrain of the province.

sets.name: Physical Education, Physical Activities & Exercises
sets.description: Definitions, legal bases and objectives of Physical Education, wellness, the health- and skill-related fitness components, safety guidelines, BMI, and the body types.

terms.definition: A course program and physiological aspect to process develop skills and knowledge through physical and mind.

It refers to an individual overall health and well-being as well as their ability to perform task and physical activities effectively and efficiently with vigour and without over fatigue and enough energy.
WHO defines it as a state of complete physical, mental and social well being and not merely the absence of disease or infirmity.
It is the ability to perform daily activities efficiently and effectively without experiencing excessive fatigue.

terms.term: The 2 Legal Bases of Physical Education
answers: International Charter of Physical Education and Sports
1986 Constitution of the Republic of the Philippines

terms.term: International Charter of Physical Education and Sports
terms.definition: UNESCO, Paris, 1978. Its Article 1, together with Recommendation 1 of the Interdisciplinary Regional Meeting of Experts (UNESCO, Brisbane Australia, 1962), states that the practice is a fundamental right for all, and this right should not be treated as different in principle from the right to adequate food, shelter and medical care.

The provision of the 1986 Constitution of the Republic of the Philippines stating: "The State shall promote Physical Education and encourage sports programs league competitions, amateur sports including training for international competition to foster self-discipline, teamwork and excellence for the development of a healthy and alert citizenry." It also provides that "All educational institutions shall undertake regular sports activities throughout the country and in cooperation with athletic club and other sectors."

terms.term: The 4 General Objectives of Physical Education

terms.definition: To improve fitness, strength, endurance, flexibility, coordination, and overall body functioning. To develop motor skills and promote healthy lifestyle habits that contribute to lifelong wellness.
To help individuals develop self-confidence, self-esteem, self-discipline, and emotional stability. To provide opportunities for stress relief, expression, and the development of positive attitudes.

It is the development and maintenance of meaningful interpersonal relationships. To foster teamwork, cooperation, leadership, sportsmanship, and respect for others. It encourages effective communication and the ability to work harmoniously with diverse groups of people.

To develop critical thinking, decision-making, problem-solving, and strategic thinking skills. To increase knowledge and understanding of health, fitness, movement principles, and the value of physical activity.

It is the positive state of well-being and capability of an individual to design personal fitness programs for improving and maintaining optimum levels of health. It is a combination of many different components (mental, social, emotional, spiritual and physical) that expand one's potential to live a quality life, to work effectively and to make a significant contribution to the society. It reflects how one feels about life as well as one's ability to function effectively, and is also described as the positive component of good health.

An essential subject that promotes the holistic development of students by integrating physical activity, health education, and wellness practices to enhance their overall quality of life. It helps students improve their physical fitness, including strength, endurance, flexibility, coordination, and motor skills, while encouraging healthy lifestyle habits such as proper nutrition, regular exercise, and adequate rest. It also contributes to mental and emotional well-being by reducing stress and building self-confidence, emotional resilience, and self-esteem. Through collaborative activities, it develops important social skills such as teamwork, communication, leadership, and respect for others. Additionally, it supports cognitive and academic performance by improving concentration and memory, while fostering discipline, responsibility, perseverance, and time management. Ultimately, it instills the value of lifelong physical activity and wellness, empowering students to lead healthy, active, and productive lives.

Regular exercise and physical activity promotes strong muscles and bones. It improves respiratory, cardiovascular health and overall health. It can also help you maintain a healthy weight, reduce your risk for type 2 diabetes, heart disease, and reduce your risk for some cancers.

Through regular exercise, the individual gains: proper growth of young bones and muscles; improved ability to avoid and recover from illnesses and accidents; better posture and appearance; a minimized stress response; maintenance of proper body weight; prevention of heart ailments; improved organic functions; a delayed aging process; feeling good and younger; and the joy of participation in any recreational or sports activity.

terms.term: The 5 Health-Related Components of Physical Fitness

terms.definition: The maximum amount of force that a muscle or group of muscles can exert in a single effort. It is important for performing tasks that require lifting, pushing, or pulling. Examples: Weightlifting, push-ups, and resistance training.

The ability of muscles to perform repeated contractions or sustain a contraction over a period of time without becoming fatigued. Examples: Sit-ups, planks, cycling, and repetitive bodyweight exercises.

This refers to the ability of the heart, lungs, and blood vessels to supply oxygen to the body during prolonged physical activity. Good levels of it improve stamina and reduce the risk of cardiovascular diseases. Examples: Walking, jogging, cycling, swimming, and dancing.

Refers to the ability of joints and muscles to move through their full range of motion. Good levels of it help prevent injuries, improve posture, and enhance physical performance. Examples: Stretching exercises, yoga, gymnastics, and dynamic warm-up routines.

Refers to the proportion of fat, muscle, bone, and other tissues that make up a person's body weight. Maintaining a healthy one is important for overall health and reducing the risk of obesity-related diseases. Examples of assessment: Body Mass Index, waist circumference, and body fat percentage measurements.

terms.term: The 10 Skill-Related Components of Physical Fitness

terms.definition: Ability to continue doing something difficult for a long time without giving up or becoming exhausted.
The ability to keep going without getting tired quickly. Strength and energy needed to sustain physical effort for long period of time.
This is the ability to exert force.
The ability to maximize effort of force of body to any movement with the use of muscle.
The ability to complete a movement or task in the shortest amount of time.
The ability to combine several different movement patterns in a single distinct movement with the use of the senses, which are a combination of body and mind.
The ability to control movement in each direction or intensity.
The ability to maximize the time going from one movement to another.
The ability to control the center of gravity of your body in relation to your support base.
The ability to respond quickly; "reflexes". The time required to respond or initiate a movement because of a given stimulus.

terms.term: The 4 Benefits of Health and Wellness

terms.definition: Regular physical activity is a healthy lifestyle. Healthy lifestyles such as proper nutrition, good posture and good body mechanics can help you look at your best.
People who engage in regular activity feel better. You can resist fatigue, are less likely to be injured and can work more efficiently.
Life is more enjoyable when you engage in regular physical activity that results in physical fitness as the key to being able to do more of the things you want to do.
Fit and active person has the capacity to help or to assist other people when they needed some help.

These guidelines include procedures that help prevent accidents or injury in Physical Education classes and activities. It should be recognized that many of these require cooperation in all class activities.

terms.term: The 5 Safety Guidelines in Physical Education Classes and Activities
answers: Always participate and give the best effort in every activity
Consideration should be given first to the safety of the student
Provide a pleasant atmosphere in which the students can work out

terms.definition: It tests for weight categories that may be associated with health issues. Formula: kilograms divided by meters squared. Example: 60 ÷ 1.70² = 60 ÷ 2.89 = 20.76.
More round or soft physique, gains and stores fat more easily, loses fat slowly, naturally slow metabolism.
Naturally muscular and athletic with shoulders wider than the hips; efficient metabolism making it easy to gain muscle and lose fat.
Slender with less muscle mass, narrow shoulders and hips with respect to height, naturally fast metabolism making it difficult to gain mass.

sets.name: Best Practices of Public Speaking
sets.description: Practical techniques for delivering an effective speech.

terms.term: 3 Foundations an Effective Speech Builds On

terms.definition: A PowerPoint guideline from the University of Leicester: slides should have no more than six bullet points, and each bullet point should be no more than six words long.

sets.name: Communication and Globalization
sets.description: Globalization, World Englishes, and culturally-sensitive, bias-free language.

terms.definition: A term used to describe how trade and technology have made the world into a more connected and interdependent place; it also captures in its scope the economic and social changes that have come about as a result.
In his book English as a Global Language (2003), he asserted that English is, in fact, the global language.

terms.term: 5 General Principles for Culturally-Sensitive and Bias-Free Language

terms.definition: One form of discrimination against a person or persons of a different race.
Class discrimination; a form of prejudice against a person or people because of their social class.
A form of discrimination against other people because of their age, or assuming that older people are less physically, intellectually, or emotionally able than other age groups.
Putting the person or people first when referring to people with disabilities, focusing on the person and not the condition.

sets.name: Communication Ethics
sets.description: Communication, ethics, and the NCA principles of ethical communication.

terms.definition: The act of transferring information from one place, person or group to another.
It enhances human worth and dignity by fostering truthfulness, fairness, responsibility, personal integrity, and respect for self and others.
Fundamental to responsible thinking, decision making, and the development of relationships and communities within and across contexts, cultures, channels, and media.

terms.term: The 4 Principles of Communication Ethics by the NCA
answers: Advocate truthfulness, accuracy, honesty, and reason
Freedom of expression, diversity of perspective, and tolerance of dissent
Condemn communication that degrades individuals and humanity
Accept responsibility for the consequences of our own communication

sets.name: Communication Models
sets.description: The classic models of communication and White's stages of oral communication.

terms.term: 5 Elements of Aristotle's Model of Communication
8 Stages of White's Model of Oral Communication

sets.name: Public Speaking
sets.description: The nature and history of public speaking, its key figures, and Philippine traditions.

terms.definition: A process of communicating purposeful information in front of an audience. It is usually face-to-face and in a formal setting where the aim of the speaker is to influence the listeners.

terms.term: 5 Periods in the History of Public Speaking

terms.definition: The Greek teacher of rhetoric who, with his student Tisias, helped citizens speak persuasively in courts of law. According to him, a basic speech has three parts, and this simple organization has endured throughout the ages.

terms.term: 3 Parts of a Basic Speech according to Corax

terms.definition: The father of debate, who made his students argue for and against issues of the day, to sharpen their reasoning skills and appreciate different sides of an issue.
Also known as the father of modern communication; wrote a treatise entitled “Rhetoric,” where he discussed the use of logos, pathos, and ethos in persuasive speaking.
Refers to ethics, meaning a speaker must radiate moral character to create an aura of trust and authority in the audience; the speaker's character and credibility.

Means supporting your speech with logical reasoning in the form of facts, figures, and evidence, and one of the most effective ways of helping the audience get to the conclusion of an argument on their own.
Refers to adding an emotional touch to your speech, so as to cater to the audience's emotions.

terms.term: 3 Modes of Persuasion discussed by Aristotle

terms.definition: The most famous orator in Ancient Greece. In the beginning he had many flaws in public speaking, chief among them his stammer and weak voice; he practiced earnestly by declaiming on the seashore with pebbles in his mouth, and his speeches were known for their deliberation and forethought.

The most famous Roman orator, whose eloquence was described as a “resistless torrent.” A statesman who argued that the teaching of rhetoric should be considered an art form useful in all practical and public affairs, and who developed the Five Canons of Rhetoric.
The Roman lawyer and educator who forwarded the idea that public speakers should be ethical; he described the ideal speaker as “a good man speaking well.”
A Christian clergyman and renowned rhetorician who continued to develop ideas and study the persuasion that had originated during the Classical Period.
Challenged the theories of the great scholars from the Classical Period and focused on logic rather than rhetoric; according to him, logic falls under two parts.

terms.term: 2 Parts of Logic according to Petrus Ramus

terms.definition: A Scottish minister and educator who used scientific and moral reasoning to understand how persuasion in speech works.
An approach that mainly focused on the delivery aspect of public speaking, namely tone of voice, gestures, body language, facial expressions, and pronunciations.
Considered public speaking as a separate field of study; communication departments have professors or instructors to teach about classical and modern rhetoric.
A training organization that aims at teaching its members public speaking skills.

In pre-colonial times, women who were allowed to speak in public for the purpose of presiding over religious rituals; along with the warriors of the community, they were the leaders of pre-colonial Philippines.
The warriors of the community who, along with the babaylans, were the leaders of pre-colonial Philippines.
A game wherein young men and women duel with each other using words when it comes to talking about love.

A poetic joust staged to honor Francisco Balagtas; like an ordinary debate except that one has to reason and argue in verse, with two master poets defending the pros and cons of an issue and a board of judges determining the winner.
A game used to entertain guests and the bereaved family during wakes.

sets.name: General Overview of Tour Guiding Concept
sets.description: History of tour guiding, core definitions, the four perspectives of tourism, and the types of tours and tour guides.

terms.definition: One of the most promising and rewarding yet difficult careers in the hospitality business; before one can pursue it, he or she must perfect particular talents and gain specialized knowledge.

A person who escorts individual visitors or people in organized tours to various places of interest while providing assistance and information for a safe, comfortable, and entertaining or appropriate tour experience.

A qualified person who accompanies people traveling through different countries and abroad to ensure the regular running of the planned trips, providing the necessary support services for its entire duration and giving significant information about the site.

terms.term: 4 Periods in the History of Tour Guiding

terms.definition: Ancient Greek term for guides meaning 'Leaders Around.'
Ancient Greek term for guides meaning 'Explainers.'
Ancient Greek term for guides meaning 'Helpers Abroad.'

terms.term: 3 Roles of the Guide in the Middle Ages

terms.definition: When young upper-class men toured Europe as part of their education, accompanied by personal tutors.
The personal tutor, also called a bear leader, who accompanied young upper-class men on the Grand Tour; expected to be multilingual and deeply knowledgeable.

terms.term: 3 Areas a Cicerone Was Expected to Know
3 Notable Travel Writers of the Modern Age
7 Importance of Tour Guiding in the Tourism Industry

terms.definition: The perspective that seeks various psychic and physical experiences and satisfactions; their nature largely determines the destinations chosen and the activities enjoyed.
The perspective of the businesses providing tourist goods and services, which see tourism as an opportunity to make a profit by supplying the goods and services that the tourist markets demand.

The perspective that sees tourism as a tool for economic development and national growth, responsible for creating policies, infrastructure, and regulations that support tourism while ensuring sustainability, safety, and cultural preservation.

The perspective that looks at how tourism affects the local people and their environment, including both positive impacts and negative effects; community support is essential for sustainable tourism development.
A type of guided tour that operates every day on a regular schedule, allowing tourists to join without needing a special booking for a custom trip.
A tour specially arranged based on the specific needs of the tourist, normally not conducted regularly.
A trip in which various tour members meet at different locations but travel to the same destination, not having known each other before joining the trip group.
A type of tour where tourists have to do a lot of walking to see the different aspects of the tour.
A visit to different museums as the main part of the tour; it could be a whole or half day of museum visits.

A tour where students visit an actual facility or company related to their topic of study, with in-house speakers delivering lectures and participants touring the company's departments to support real observation and gain hands-on experience.
A tour of different ecological areas with a focus on environmental protection; it typically includes walking, hiking, and interaction with the locals.
A visit to religious and worship places like churches and mission houses.
A tour conducted for a specific type of tourist, such as going to a faith healer, shooting a film, agri-tourism, or any other tour of special interest.
Conducts one- or several-hour tours of a certain location, site, or building; a museum curator is another type of this guide.
A volunteer guide who normally works in a small museum and often works for free.
Points out and comments on the highlights of a city, usually from a motor coach, minibus, or van, but sometimes as part of a walking tour.
A city guide who does double duty by driving and narrating.
A guide whose services may be available at a reasonable price, taking a small number of individuals on their own exclusive tour.
Someone whose expertise or skills are highly unique; for example, adventure guides lead unusual, physically demanding tour experiences.
A 'specialist' who boards motor coaches to provide an informed overview of the city to be toured.
A person competent enough to give information on a certain area or locality.
A person who specializes in a certain field such as botany, architecture, or zoology.
A person fluent in two or more foreign languages.
A person permanently connected with a travel agency, paid monthly.
A person who is not permanently connected with a travel agency, paid per trip.
A guide with a native or aboriginal background who has a deep understanding of their culture.
A guide who is a specialist in environmental knowledge.

sets.name: Qualities of an Ideal Tour Guide
sets.description: The qualities of an ideal tour guide and the rules in tour guiding.

terms.term: Qualities of an Ideal Tour Guide

terms.definition: The most important characteristic of successful guides: a keen interest in the subject matter and the travelers that enables the guide to withstand mentally and physically exhausting experiences.
Being friendly and open in many types of situations and encouraging others to do the same, since guides are in the business of meeting, welcoming, and working with strangers.
A quality developed through self-esteem combined with experience; it enables guides to carry on their duties effectively, put people at ease, and help create enjoyable experiences.

The belief in initiating change and the readiness to take responsibility for it, often paired with strong optimism; it is essential for effective leadership in challenging and unpredictable situations that require swift solutions.
A wide range of human understanding; successful guides are compassionate, respectful, tactful, and observant, and are sensitive to the needs of tourists.
The ability to be adaptable and patient, essential in the travel business where even the best planned itinerary may go wrong.
Genuineness and honesty; most tourists trust guides who are honest and sincere.
The quality reflecting that first impressions are often lasting and frequently based on one's physical appearance.
The quality that can bring people closer together, put them at ease, and help make travel enjoyable instead of stressful and unpleasant.
Having a well-rounded understanding of various topics; because subject areas vary by region, guides must research and familiarize themselves with the local educational programs specific to each area.

The ability to communicate well, without which even the most knowledgeable and sensitive person will not succeed as a guide; it includes articulation, eye contact, gestures, and a clear, pleasant speaking voice.
Time management and organization skills, important for sticking to a schedule despite delays, meeting appointments, interacting with guests, and handling the paperwork behind every tour.
The ability to make quick decisions, which may be unpopular or costly, especially during a tour emergency that requires spending the company's money or changing an itinerary.
Needed because guiding is strenuous, entailing a good deal of walking, long and irregular hours, different diets, and the stress of always being in the spotlight.
A strong sense of ethics essential to success; guides have a moral responsibility to themselves, their employers, colleagues, travelers, and even to their regions.

Defined as a personal magic of leadership arousing popular loyalty or enthusiasm; it is the combination of the many traits a guide should possess, and some level of it is evident in most successful guides.
The rule that tour guides must act professionally at all times by being polite, respectful, and responsible.
The rule that being on time and well-prepared ensures smooth tour operations and avoids delays.
The rule that only verified and correct information must be shared with tourists.
The rule that the safety of tourists must always be the top priority in every activity.
The rule that tour guides must respect local traditions and encourage responsible tourism behavior.
The rule that a tour guide must ensure that all tourists are organized, accounted for, and safe.
The rule that clear and understandable communication is essential for effective guiding.
The rule that all local rules, tourism regulations, and company policies must be strictly followed.
The rule that tour guides should provide excellent service and handle concerns professionally.

sets.name: The Tour and the Tour Guide
sets.description: The elements of tour guiding and the five roles of tour guides.

terms.definition: Before becoming a tour guide, a person must undergo a training program and acquire a license to practice legally.
A form of product that is intangible, perishable, and one whose production and consumption happen simultaneously.
One of the tour guide's roles: to educate tourists about the meanings and significance of cultural attractions.
The tangible parts a tour guide combines with service to create the total tourism experience for clients; they include hotel facilities, transport vehicles, parks, and museums.

The role in which the guide must have many of the skills required of leaders and managers in business, such as a willingness to assume responsibility, the ability to organize and make wise decisions, the strength to respond to emergencies, and the skill to coordinate many administrative tasks simultaneously.
The role reflecting that if travel is a classroom and travelers are students, then the guides are these, teaching subjects such as history, architecture, and geography.
The role for which tour guides are frequently called ambassadors, because of the personal connections they create between places and visitors and the diplomatic skills required of them.
The role of one who enjoys people and creates an environment in which people feel comfortable, is sensitive to others' needs, and has a genuine interest in travelers as the most important element.

The role as a channel link, considered the most important; it cannot be separated from any other role and emphasizes the importance of the visitor, the local culture, and the travel experience rather than the guide's performance.
The component of leadership that includes navigating, providing special access, shepherding, and maintaining control.
The component of leadership that includes tension management, integrating the group, keeping good humor and morale, and entertaining members of the tour.
